metadata.summary: A step-by-step playbook for building a product or professional community with lasting engagement via the SEED-NURTURE-SCALE-MONETIZE model with core rituals and health metrics.

content.title: Community Building
content.summary: A step-by-step playbook for building a product or professional community with lasting engagement. The four-stage model is: Seed (50 hand-picked members) → Nurture (rituals: daily standup, weekly office hours, monthly AMA) → Scale (member-to-member connections via pairing, breakout groups) → Monetize (community as product feature). Core rule: one platform, pre-seeded with 5-10 planted conversations before the first invitation wave.

why.p: Communities create acquisition moats: they generate organic content, handle peer support, and become the best sales force. Without rituals, communities become ghost towns — members visit once, find nothing happening, and never return. The DAU/MAU ratio is the leading health indicator: below 20% signals ritual failure. Most community failures stem from launching empty or opening too many spaces before existing ones are active.

list.item: Drafting launch materials: welcome messages, guidelines, onboarding sequences, ritual prompts.
Generating invitation scripts tailored to specific member profiles.
Producing weekly ritual content (standups, AMA question lists, challenge briefs, roundup newsletters).
Analyzing community health metrics from platform exports to identify churn risk.
Writing community guidelines and moderation playbooks.
Real-time community moderation — requires human judgment on nuance, context, and tone.
Automated mass-invitation campaigns — platforms detect and ban bots; personal invitations require human send.
Communities with fewer than 10 seeded members — agent-generated content in an empty community reads hollow.
Replacing the community manager role entirely — member trust is built through perceived human presence.

rule.statement: Progress through stages in order — do not open invitations until the SEED stage has 5-10 planted conversations and 50 hand-picked members confirmed.
rule.rationale: Launching empty is the single most common community failure. Visitors who arrive and see no activity leave immediately and never return. The first impression cannot be undone.

list.item: SEED: invite 50 hand-picked members, plant 5-10 conversations before anyone else arrives.
NURTURE: establish daily, weekly, and monthly rituals; personal welcome within 48 hours of each new join.
SCALE: enable member-to-member connections via pairing, breakout groups, collaboration board.
MONETIZE: community access becomes a product feature or paid tier.

rule.statement: Pick exactly one platform and build depth there before considering others — multi-platform splits both member attention and your management capacity.
rule.rationale: Each platform requires a different engagement style and moderation approach. Running two platforms at 50% capacity produces two ghost towns instead of one thriving community.

list.item: Discord: real-time, rich features; best for tech, gaming, crypto; noisy, hard to search.
Slack: familiar, searchable; best for B2B professional communities; expensive at scale.
Circle: structured, monetizable; best for courses and memberships; less sticky.
Reddit: SEO and organic growth; less control over experience.

rule.statement: Define the community's value proposition across all three pillars (Access, Connection, Content) before writing the first invitation — members join for one pillar and stay for another.
rule.rationale: Communities that offer only one pillar churn when the founding hook loses novelty. The Access pillar (founder availability) is highest at launch but cannot scale; Connection and Content pillars must be established before Access fades.

list.item: Access: to you, experts, or exclusive information not available elsewhere.
Connection: to peers with similar goals, industry, or stage.
Content: educational, entertaining, or actionable material on a consistent schedule.

section.name: seeding-first-members

rule.statement: Hand-pick the first 50 members from existing engaged contacts — email subscribers, early users, engaged commenters — not the general public.
rule.rationale: Quality of the founding cohort determines the culture and conversation norms for the entire community. Strangers with no prior engagement produce transactional interactions; engaged contacts produce genuine dialogue.

example.title: Invitation script for the founding cohort
example.code:
Hey [Name],
I'm building a small community of [description].
I loved your [recent post/project/comment] about [topic].
Would you like early access? We're keeping it small (~50 people) to maintain quality conversations.
[Link]
example.note: Personalized script targeting engaged existing contacts.

section.reference: Pre-launch / Launch Week / First Month checklist

section.name: ritual-cadence

rule.statement: Establish at least one daily ritual, one weekly ritual, and one monthly ritual — vary wording each week even if the structure repeats to prevent ritual fatigue.
rule.rationale: Rituals create the habit of checking the community. A member who skips two rituals in a row is unlikely to return; daily rituals make the community part of the daily workflow before the habit window closes.

rule.statement: Make rituals low-friction: a "what are you working on today?" standup beats a weekly essay prompt for participation rate by 5-10x.
rule.rationale: High-friction rituals punish busy members and create participation guilt. Low-friction rituals include everyone, including lurkers who would otherwise disengage.

list.item: Daily: morning standup thread (what are you working on?), win/loss sharing.
Weekly: office hours with you, member spotlight, weekly roundup newsletter.
Monthly: guest AMA, challenge or hackathon, retrospective thread.

section.reference: Community guidelines template with purpose, values, and moderation policy.

rule.statement: Trigger a personal welcome within 48 hours of every new member joining — the first 48 hours are the highest-churn window.
rule.rationale: New members who receive no personal contact in the first 48 hours have a 60-70% probability of never posting. A personal welcome converts lurkers into participants at the moment of highest receptivity.

list.item: Intro threads: new members introduce themselves with a structured prompt.
Pairing: match members with similar goals for 1:1 conversations.
Breakout groups: cohorts of 5-10 people around shared interests or stages.
Collaboration board: members post help-wanted and help-offered items.

rule.statement: Do not open new channels or spaces until existing ones are consistently active — empty spaces signal low community health to every new visitor.
rule.rationale: New members calibrate community quality by scanning visible activity. Five active channels look healthier than fifteen half-empty ones.

section.name: health-metrics

rule.statement: Track DAU/MAU (target 20%, great 40%+) and post rate (target 10% of members posting monthly, great 25%+) as the primary health signals — total member count is a vanity metric.
rule.rationale: A community of 500 with 40% DAU/MAU is healthier and more monetizable than a community of 5,000 with 5% DAU/MAU. Growth strategy should optimize for ratio, not absolute count.

list.item: DAU/MAU: good 20%, great 40%+.
Post rate: good 10% of members, great 25%+.
Average response time to posts: good <4 hours, great <1 hour.
30-day retention: good 70%, great 85%+.
NPS: good 30, great 50+.

section.reference: Python health scorer from Discord/Circle activity exports.

antipattern.description: Over-moderating: removing posts aggressively or requiring approval before posts are visible.
antipattern.reason: Heavy moderation kills spontaneity and signals distrust. Members self-censor when they feel monitored. Light touch moderation with clear guidelines produces better outcomes.

antipattern.description: Only the founder posting — treating the community as a broadcast channel.
antipattern.reason: If members can only receive content rather than contribute it, the community is a newsletter. Communities require member-generated content to produce the connection value that justifies membership.

antipattern.description: Growing headcount faster than community health metrics support.
antipattern.reason: Rapid growth in a community with low DAU/MAU dilutes conversation quality and makes new members feel like they joined after the good times. Quality of engagement must precede scale of membership.

list.item: Seed the community with 5-10 planted conversations before inviting the first wave — an empty platform kills momentum before it starts.
Rituals must be low-friction: a "what are you working on today?" standup beats a weekly essay prompt for participation rate.
The first 48 hours after a new member joins are the highest-churn window — trigger a personal welcome within that window.
Identify and invest in 3-5 ambassadors early; they create a self-sustaining culture before the founder steps back.
Do not open new channels/spaces until existing ones are active — too many empty spaces signal low community health.
Track reply rate per post, not just post count; a community where 30% of posts get zero replies is dying.
Platform rate limits: Discord bot API and Circle API have per-minute call limits; batch analytics pulls must be throttled.
Privacy in analysis: Member activity data often contains email addresses and real names — scrub before feeding into external LLM calls.
Ritual fatigue: Agent-generated ritual templates repeated verbatim week after week feel mechanical; vary wording and theme even if the structure repeats.
Moderation edge cases: Agent moderation policy documents will not cover every real scenario; the first novel moderation call must be made by a human and then documented as precedent.
Platform churn: Threads-linked communities (Threads/Instagram) have different feature sets than established platforms; agent advice based on Discord/Slack may not translate.

template.purpose: Guidelines template: Purpose / Values / Rules / Encouragements / Moderation policy.
Pre-launch / Launch Week / First Month checklist.
Score community health from Discord/Circle export (DAU/MAU, post rate, churn risk).
